mission.title: Install and Configure Strimzi Kafka Operator
mission.description: Strimzi Kafka Operator allows you to run Apache Kafka on Kubernetes, simplifying the deployment and management of Kafka clusters. It is ideal for users looking to integrate Kafka into their Kubernetes environments with ease. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and the complexity of managing Kafka configurations.

steps.title: Add the Strimzi Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Strimzi Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration, allowing you to install the Strimzi Kafka Operator.

```bash

helm repo add strimzi https://strimzi.io/charts/

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Strimzi repository.
> Updates your local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the Strimzi Kafka Operator
steps.description:
This step installs the Strimzi Kafka Operator using Helm, specifying the namespace and version.

```bash

kubectl create namespace kafka-namespace

helm install strimzi strimzi/strimzi-kafka-operator --namespace kafka-namespace --version 0.44.0

```

> Creates a namespace for the Kafka Operator.
> Installs the Strimzi Kafka Operator in the created namespace.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Strimzi Kafka Operator is running correctly by listing the pods in the Kafka namespace.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kafka-namespace

```

> Lists all pods in the kafka-namespace to verify the operator is running.

steps.title: Create a Kafka cluster
steps.description:
This step creates a Kafka cluster using a custom resource definition (CRD) to define the cluster configuration.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/strimzi/strimzi-kafka-operator/0.44.0/examples/kafka/kafka-persistent.yaml

```

> Deploys a Kafka cluster with persistent storage.

steps.title: Verify the Kafka cluster
steps.description:
This step checks if the Kafka cluster is running correctly by listing the Kafka pods.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kafka-namespace

```

> Lists all pods in the kafka-namespace to verify the Kafka cluster is running.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the Strimzi Kafka Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Strimzi Kafka Operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this will delete the Kafka cluster and any data stored in it.

```bash

helm uninstall strimzi --namespace kafka-namespace

kubectl delete namespace kafka-namespace

```

> Uninstalls the Strimzi Kafka Operator.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Strimzi Kafka Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your Kafka data. This step upgrades the Strimzi Kafka Operator to a newer version.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kafka-namespace -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}' | xargs kubectl drain --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

helm upgrade strimzi strimzi/strimzi-kafka-operator --namespace kafka-namespace --version 0.44.0

kubectl get pods -n kafka-namespace

```

> Drains the Kafka pods before upgrading.
> Upgrades the Strimzi Kafka Operator to the specified version.
> Verifies that the Kafka Operator pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get pods -n kafka-namespace -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}' | xargs kubectl drain --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to Kafka broker'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the Kafka broker is not running or is misconfigured.

**Fix:**
Check the Kafka broker logs using 'kubectl logs <broker-pod-name> -n kafka-namespace' for more details.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Kafka cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Kafka cluster is still initializing or has failed to start.

**Fix:**
Run 'kubectl describe kafka <kafka-cluster-name> -n kafka-namespace' to check the status and events.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'No resources found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Kafka cluster CRD was not applied correctly.

**Fix:**
Ensure the CRD file was applied correctly and check the namespace with 'kubectl get kafka -n kafka-namespace'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Permission denied'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may happen if the RBAC permissions are not set correctly for the Kafka Operator.

**Fix:**
Check the RBAC roles and bindings with 'kubectl get roles,rolebindings -n kafka-namespace'.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Kafka 3.4.0, improved metrics collection.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the use of 'KafkaUser' CRD in favor of 'KafkaUserV2'.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 0.43, ensure to migrate existing KafkaUser CRDs to the new version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kafka cluster running with the command 'kubectl get kafka -n kafka-namespace' returning the status as 'Ready'.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary resources.
